section_path: About Cognizant > What we do
text:
[About Cognizant | About Cognizant > What we do]
Cognizant uses expertise proven and tested around the globe to help clients get ahead of challenges, sense opportunities sooner, and outpace change.  
- Transform experiences: hyper-personalized experiences that connect faster, generate growth, and build brand loyalty.
- Reimagine processes: process automation and technology to act with insight, precision, and speed.
- Modernize technology: software built to learn and deploy processing power, unlocked in the cloud to create value in every byte of data.  
The aim is to move beyond just running a business and turn it into one that sees the next best action and acts as if on intuition, in the moments that matter.

section_path: About Cognizant > Culture and values
text:
[About Cognizant | About Cognizant > Culture and values]
Each member of the team takes responsibility for creating a culture that enables exceptional outcomes.
- What we value: help imagine, build, and implement technologies to keep clients aware and responsive.

- Who we are: people engineer impact with clients, communities, and colleagues.

text:

section_path: Cognizant — Agentic AI > Focus on the agentic enterprise
text:
[Cognizant — Agentic AI | Cognizant — Agentic AI > Focus on the agentic enterprise]
Cognizant is centering its offerings on agentic AI — systems where autonomous AI
agents and humans work together across business functions to accelerate outcomes.
The company's goal is to give enterprises a composable, platform-agnostic path to
becoming "agentic enterprises," moving from proofs of concept to large-scale
production deployments.

section_path: Cognizant — Agentic AI > Cognizant Agent Foundry
text:
[Cognizant — Agentic AI | Cognizant — Agentic AI > Cognizant Agent Foundry]
Launched July 10, 2025, Cognizant Agent Foundry helps enterprises design, deploy,
and orchestrate autonomous AI agents at scale. It provides a composable,
platform-agnostic pathway so humans and AI agents can work in concert across
functions and unlock business value from AI.

section_path: Cognizant — Agentic AI > Neuro AI Multi-Agent Accelerator
text:
[Cognizant — Agentic AI | Cognizant — Agentic AI > Neuro AI Multi-Agent Accelerator]
A no-code multi-agent orchestration framework (part of the Neuro platform suite)
that lets organizations design, deploy, and scale networks of collaborating
agents. Cognizant open-sourced it in 2025 for research and academic use.

section_path: Cognizant — Agentic AI > Cross-platform interoperability
text:
[Cognizant — Agentic AI | Cognizant — Agentic AI > Cross-platform interoperability]
- June 18, 2026 — ServiceNow AI Agents now interoperate with the Neuro AI
Multi-Agent Accelerator, giving enterprises a unified environment to orchestrate
agents across the platforms they already run.
- June 2026 — Expanded alliance with CrowdStrike to secure the agentic enterprise.
- June 2026 — Collaboration with Snowflake to use Cortex-powered intelligent agents.

section_path: Cognizant — Agentic AI > AI governance for agents
text:
[Cognizant — Agentic AI | Cognizant — Agentic AI > AI governance for agents]
Cognizant pairs agentic AI with governance. In June 2026 it integrated Neuro AI
Trust with ServiceNow to deliver continuous AI assurance, and it emphasizes
responsible, governed AI (Cognizant is the first IT services provider certified to
ISO/IEC 42001:2023, the AI management system standard).

section_path: Cognizant — Agentic AI > AI Lab
text:
[Cognizant — Agentic AI | Cognizant — Agentic AI > AI Lab]
The Cognizant AI Lab advances decision AI and multi-agent systems. In 2025 its
research shifted toward systems that can plan, act, adapt, and collaborate in real
decision environments, producing tools such as Neuro SAN and published academic work.

section_path: Cognizant — Company Profile, Recognition, and Acquisitions > At a glance
text:
[Cognizant — Company Profile, Recognition, and Acquisitions | Cognizant — Company Profile, Recognition, and Acquisitions > At a glance]
- Cognizant Technology Solutions Corporation (Nasdaq: CTSH) is an American multinational IT consulting and professional services company headquartered in Teaneck, New Jersey.
- Founded in 1994 in Chennai, India, as an in-house technology unit of Dun & Bradstreet; began serving external clients in 1996 and went public in 1998.
- One of the world's leading professional services companies, organized around industry verticals and technology/operations horizontals.
- FY2025 revenue: $21.1 billion (up 7.0% year-over-year), up from $19.7 billion in FY2024.
- About 350,000–357,600 employees (varies by reporting date through Q1 2026).

doc_title: Cognizant — Company Profile, Recognition, and Acquisitions

text:
[Cognizant — Company Profile, Recognition, and Acquisitions | Cognizant — Company Profile, Recognition, and Acquisitions > Business and delivery model]
- Cognizant pioneered a global delivery model that blends local, onshore client management with large-scale offshore development and delivery centers, emphasizing scalable, cost-effective IT services.
- The model pairs deep industry/domain expertise (vertical units) with technology and process capabilities (horizontal units), now anchored by its "AI builder" strategy and the Neuro AI platform suite.
- A large share of the workforce is based in India (Chennai, Hyderabad, and Bengaluru are the largest centers), with delivery centers across the Americas, Europe, and Asia-Pacific.

section_path: Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite
text:
[Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite | Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite]
Source: https://www.cognizant.com/us/en/services/cognizant-platforms (and Cognizant newsroom, 2023–2026)

section_path: Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Overview
text:
[Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite | Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Overview]
Cognizant Neuro® is the company's family of AI-led platforms that sit at the
center of its "AI builder" strategy. The suite orchestrates AI, automation, and
agents across the enterprise technology stack so clients can move from isolated
AI pilots to scaled, production-grade adoption. The Neuro AI platform underpins
Cognizant's data, generative AI, and agentic AI services.

section_path: Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Neuro AI
text:
[Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite | Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Neuro AI]
Neuro AI is Cognizant's enterprise platform for adopting generative and agentic
AI. It helps enterprises build, deploy, and orchestrate AI use cases on top of
their existing data, models, and infrastructure. Built with NVIDIA AI, the
platform delivers specialized industry large language models, reasoning agents,
and digital twins, and orchestrates across the enterprise stack.  
Key components and research platforms include:
- Neuro AI Decisioning — decision-focused AI for complex business situations.
- Neuro AI System of Agent Networks (Neuro SAN) — a multi-agent orchestration
platform from the Cognizant AI Lab for designing networks of collaborating agents.

section_path: Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Neuro AI Multi-Agent Accelerator
text:
[Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite | Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Neuro AI Multi-Agent Accelerator]
Announced January 2025, the Neuro AI Multi-Agent Accelerator is a no-code
framework with pre-built reference agent networks that let businesses rapidly
prototype, customize, and scale multi-agent systems. It is part of a broader
Multi-Agent Services Suite for transforming business processes. In May 2025
Cognizant open-sourced the accelerator for research and academic use, and it
later won a Global AI Award for best AI product or service.

section_path: Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Neuro IT Operations
text:
[Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite | Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Neuro IT Operations]
Launched in 2023, Neuro IT Operations is an AI-driven automation platform that
reduces the complexity and operating cost of enterprise infrastructure and
technology, enabling an automation-first approach to IT operations.

section_path: Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Neuro Cybersecurity
text:
[Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite | Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Neuro Cybersecurity]
Introduced in October 2024, Neuro Cybersecurity is an AI-powered platform that
integrates and orchestrates point security solutions across the enterprise to
improve cyber resilience and risk management for the AI era.

section_path: Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Cognizant Flowsource
text:
[Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite | Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Cognizant Flowsource]
Announced February 2024, Flowsource is a generative-AI-enabled platform for
modern software engineering. It connects all stages of the software development
lifecycle and brings together digital assets and tools so engineering teams can
deliver higher-quality code faster, with more control and transparency.

section_path: Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Neuro AI Trust
text:
[Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite | Cognizant Neuro — AI Platform Suite > Neuro AI Trust]
Neuro AI Trust provides continuous AI assurance infrastructure built for
enterprise scale. In June 2026 Cognizant integrated Neuro AI Trust with
ServiceNow to support operationalized, governed AI across the enterprise.

section_path: Cognizant — Company History and Overview > Snapshot
text:
[Cognizant — Company History and Overview | Cognizant — Company History and Overview > Snapshot]
- Full name: Cognizant Technology Solutions Corporation
- Type: Public company (Nasdaq: CTSH, Class A); component of the Nasdaq-100 and S&P 500
- Industry: Information technology, consulting, outsourcing
- Founded: January 26, 1994, in Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India
- Founder: Kumar Mahadeva (with Srini Raju as co-founding CEO/MD)
- Predecessor: Dun & Bradstreet
- Headquarters: Teaneck, New Jersey, U.S.
- CEO: Ravi Kumar S (Ravi Kumar Singisetti), since January 2023
- FY2025 revenue: ~$21.1 billion; net income ~$2.23 billion; total assets ~$20.7 billion
- Employees: ~350,000+ (≈349,800 as of September 2025)

section_path: Cognizant — Company History and Overview > History and milestones
text:
[Cognizant — Company History and Overview | Cognizant — Company History and Overview > History and milestones]
- 1994: Established in Chennai as Dun & Bradstreet Satyam Software (DBSS), a 76:24 joint venture between Dun & Bradstreet and Satyam Computers. Began with about 50 employees as Dun & Bradstreet's in-house technology unit.
- 1996: Began pursuing customers beyond Dun & Bradstreet. Dun & Bradstreet spun off several subsidiaries to form Cognizant Corporation.
- 1997: DBSS renamed itself Cognizant Technology Solutions. Dun & Bradstreet bought Satyam's 24% stake.
- 1998: Headquarters moved to the United States; Kumar Mahadeva named CEO. The parent Cognizant Corporation split into IMS Health and Nielsen Media Research; Cognizant became a public subsidiary of IMS Health, which conducted an IPO of Cognizant stock in June 1998 (listed on NASDAQ).
- 2003: IMS Health sold its entire 56% stake. Kumar Mahadeva resigned as CEO and was replaced by Lakshmi Narayanan. The services portfolio expanded into BPO and business consulting.
- 2004: Added to the NASDAQ-100 Index.
- 2006: Francisco D'Souza became CEO. Moved from the S&P 400 to the S&P 500.
- 2011: Became a Fortune 500 company.
- 2014: Acquired healthcare IT services provider TriZetto Corp for $2.7 billion.
- 2019: Brian Humphries became CEO (replacing Francisco D'Souza).
- 2023: Ravi Kumar S (former Infosys president) named CEO, succeeding Brian Humphries.
- 2024: Announced partnership with Microsoft to extend generative AI and Copilot adoption; acquired Belcan (digital engineering).
- 2026: Completed acquisition of 3Cloud (Microsoft Azure services).

section_path: Cognizant — Company History and Overview > Controversies (for completeness)
text:
[Cognizant — Company History and Overview | Cognizant — Company History and Overview > Controversies (for completeness)]
Over the years Cognizant has faced various legal and labor-related matters, including: a U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act investigation (2016, related to payments in India) that led to leadership changes; an Indian dividend distribution tax dispute; H-1B visa and wage-related cases in the U.S.; a discrimination lawsuit (a 2024 retrial found against the company); and widely reported poor working conditions for Facebook content moderators at U.S. sites (2019), after which Cognizant announced it would exit parts of the content moderation business in 2020.

doc_title: Cognizant — Executive Bios and Leadership Thinking
section_path: Cognizant — Executive Bios and Leadership Thinking > Ravi Kumar S — Chief Executive Officer
text:
[Cognizant — Executive Bios and Leadership Thinking | Cognizant — Executive Bios and Leadership Thinking > Ravi Kumar S — Chief Executive Officer]
- Appointed CEO in January 2023. Sets strategic direction, promotes a "client-first" culture, focuses on sustainable growth and long-term shareholder value.
- Named to the TIME AI 100 (2025) as an influential leader in AI.
- Prior: President of Infosys (2016–2022), led the global services organization and chaired Infosys BPM. Earlier roles at PwC, Cambridge Technology Partners, Oracle, and Sapient. Began his career as a nuclear scientist at the Bhabha Atomic Research Center (India).
- External roles: board of TransUnion; board of governors, New York Academy of Sciences; Chair of the AI working committee and board member of the US Chamber of Commerce; board of the US-India Strategic Partnership Forum (USISPF).
- Education: MBA, Xavier Institute of Management (India).
- Stated thinking: champions the "AI builder" strategy; frames AI as augmenting people and tasks (not just replacing roles); emphasizes scaling AI across the enterprise and returning Cognizant to the "winner's circle."

doc_title: Cognizant — Executive Bios and Leadership Thinking
section_path: Cognizant — Executive Bios and Leadership Thinking > Jatin Dalal — Chief Financial Officer
text:
[Cognizant — Executive Bios and Leadership Thinking | Cognizant — Executive Bios and Leadership Thinking > Jatin Dalal — Chief Financial Officer]
- Joined Cognizant December 2023. Oversees worldwide FP&A, accounting/controllership, tax, treasury, internal audit, corporate development, investor relations, and enterprise risk.
- Prior: President and CFO of Wipro (CFO 2015–2019; earlier CFO of Wipro's IT Business 2011–2015); led Wipro Ventures ($300M strategic investment arm). Began career at General Electric (1999), joined Wipro 2002.
- Credentials: Chartered Accountant, Cost Accountant, Chartered Management Accountant, CFA; Wharton Advanced Management Program; Stanford Advanced Computer Security certificate; engineering degree (NIT Surat) and finance/international business diploma (NMIMS Mumbai).
- Stated thinking: emphasizes operational rigor, >100% free-cash-flow conversion, margin expansion, and disciplined capital return.

- Leads AI Research Labs; advances state-of-the-art AI, builds differentiated AI features into offerings, and leads AI-for-good initiatives.
- Prior: cofounder and CEO of Sentient (core tech behind a very large distributed AI system) and founder of the first AI-driven hedge fund (Sentient Investment Management). Senior director of engineering at Sybase iAnywhere; cofounder/CTO of Dejima — primary inventor of the agent-oriented technology behind Apple's Siri.
- Credentials: 50+ papers and 39 issued US patents; expert in NLP, machine learning, evolutionary algorithms, distributed AI. Ph.D. in Artificial Intelligence, Kyushu University (Japan).

section_path: Cognizant Reports Fourth Quarter and Full-Year 2025 Results
text:
[Cognizant Reports Fourth Quarter and Full-Year 2025 Results | Cognizant Reports Fourth Quarter and Full-Year 2025 Results]
Source: https://news.cognizant.com/2026-02-04-Cognizant-Reports-Fourth-Quarter-and-Full-Year-2025-Results
Date: February 4, 2026 (Teaneck, N.J.)

doc_title: Cognizant Reports Fourth Quarter and Full-Year 2025 Results
section_path: Cognizant Reports Fourth Quarter and Full-Year 2025 Results > Headline highlights
text:
[Cognizant Reports Fourth Quarter and Full-Year 2025 Results | Cognizant Reports Fourth Quarter and Full-Year 2025 Results > Headline highlights]
- Q4 2025 revenue of $5.3 billion, up 4.9% year-over-year (3.8% in constant currency).
- Full-year 2025 revenue of $21.1 billion, up 7.0% year-over-year (6.4% in constant currency).
- Full-year operating margin of 16.1%, up 140 bps year-over-year; Adjusted Operating Margin of 15.8%, up 50 bps.
- Full-year GAAP EPS of $4.56 (up 1%); Adjusted EPS of $5.28 (up 11%).
- Trailing 12-month bookings of $28.4 billion, up 5% year-over-year (book-to-bill ~1.3x); 28 large deals signed in 2025 (12 in Q4).
- In 2026, expects to return $1.6 billion to shareholders via buybacks and dividends, including $1 billion of repurchases.
- Cash dividend increased 6.5% to $0.33 per share for Q1 2026.
- 2026 constant currency revenue growth guidance: 4.0% to 6.5%.
- 2026 Adjusted Operating Margin guidance: 15.9% to 16.1% (10 to 30 bps expansion).  
CEO Ravi Kumar S noted Cognizant has over 350,000 employees and that 2025 was a defining year as it put its "AI builder strategy" in motion and returned to the "winner's circle" two years ahead of target, with large-deal TCV growth of nearly 50% year-over-year. CFO Jatin Dalal cited free cash flow conversion above 100% of net income and $2 billion returned to shareholders.

section_path: Responsible AI > Overview
text:
[Responsible AI | Responsible AI > Overview]
Cognizant is committed to promoting ethical, safe, and trustworthy AI aligned with its values and global standards. Through Cognizant Trust (its responsible AI / RAI framework), it works to ensure AI systems are secure, reliable, and scalable.  
Three strategic drivers:
- AI regulation and compliance: governance to stay ahead of evolving global AI laws and reduce compliance risk.
- Market trust and performance: transparent, auditable systems aligned with performance metrics.
- Business growth and opportunity: scaling responsible AI in high-demand markets.

section_path: Responsible AI > Responsible AI services
text:
[Responsible AI | Responsible AI > Responsible AI services]
- RAI readiness and governance services: strategy, governance, compliance assessments, roadmaps, operating models aligned to ISO 42001 and NIST AI RMF.
- RAI implementation services: deploy controls, tools, and frameworks; system integration, automation, enterprise-wide monitoring.
- Monitoring and operational support: continuous monitoring, performance oversight, recourse pathways, incident response playbooks.
- Lifecycle assurance and testing: testing for bias, performance, traceability, and responsible system decommissioning.
- Audit and regulatory alignment: audit readiness, documentation, frameworks conforming to evolving AI laws.
- Trustworthy and sustainable AI strategy: responsible growth roadmaps embedding trust into every stage of AI adoption.

doc_title: Cognizant — Strategy, Business Plan and Future Goals
section_path: Cognizant — Strategy, Business Plan and Future Goals > Core strategy: the "AI builder"
text:
[Cognizant — Strategy, Business Plan and Future Goals | Cognizant — Strategy, Business Plan and Future Goals > Core strategy: the "AI builder"]
Cognizant positions itself as an "AI builder," building the bridge from AI investment to enterprise value by delivering full-stack, enterprise-grade AI solutions. The strategy combines:
- Proprietary platforms: the Cognizant Neuro AI suite (Neuro AI Engineering, Neuro AI Multi-Agent Accelerator, Neuro IT Operations, Neuro Cybersecurity), plus Flowsource and Skygrade.
- Deep industry/domain expertise across verticals (Health Sciences, Financial Services, Products & Resources, CMT).
- A strengthened partnership ecosystem (Microsoft, Google Cloud, AWS, Anthropic, NVIDIA-class alliances, ServiceNow, Adobe, and 85+ strategic alliances).
- Disciplined M&A to add capability (Belcan in 2024 for digital engineering; 3Cloud in 2026 for Azure/AI).  
CEO Ravi Kumar S framed 2025 as the year Cognizant "put our AI builder strategy in motion" and returned to the "winner's circle" (industry-leading growth) two years ahead of the target set at its Investor Day. Speaking at the World Economic Forum in Davos in early 2026, Kumar described the first three years of his tenure as stabilization and the next phase as "bold bets" — including AI, outcome-based pricing, and a possible India IPO. He has noted the company closes four to five deals of over $100 million each quarter.

doc_title: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work
section_path: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work
text:
[Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work | Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work]
Sources: Cognizant Insights ("The Work Ahead" / research library, "New work, new world 2026", "The skills reset" blog), and reporting from Fortune (June 2026). Content rephrased for compliance; see links for originals.

doc_title: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work
section_path: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > "New work, new world" research (flagship AI-and-jobs study)
text:
[Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work | Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > "New work, new world" research (flagship AI-and-jobs study)]
- 2023 edition (with Oxford Economics): predicted that by 2032, ~90% of jobs would be affected by generative AI, with roughly 10% facing transformational change. The headline message: businesses that invest in people will unlock AI's full power.
- 2026 update ("New work, new world 2026: How AI is reshaping work faster than expected"): the disruption is arriving faster and broader than predicted. Cognizant's data shows ~93% of jobs are already "AI-capable," and ~30% now face fundamental/existential change. In effect, the 2032 threshold was reached around 2026, roughly six years early.
- AI "exposure scores" rose sharply across the board: e.g., construction roles moved from ~4% to ~12%, while CEO/C-suite exposure jumped from ~25% to ~60%, showing white-collar and leadership roles are far from immune.
- Related framing: AI can already perform tasks worth on the order of $4.5 trillion of US labor, but capturing that value requires intentional skilling, context, and focus (Cognizant argues there is no near-term "AI bubble" burst).
- Cognizant research leaders associated with this work include Ollie O'Donoghue (head of research) and Sushant Warikoo (chief business officer of AI).

doc_title: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work
section_path: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > Core stance: tasks, not roles
text:
[Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work | Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > Core stance: tasks, not roles]
- Cognizant's central argument is that AI changes the nature of tasks far more often than it eliminates entire roles. As tasks evolve, human judgment, adaptability, and contextual understanding become more valuable, not less.
- Mid-career and experienced professionals are pivotal: their institutional knowledge and domain expertise, amplified by AI, become a performance accelerator. Concentrating AI use among a few specialists creates capability bottlenecks.

doc_title: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work
section_path: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > "The skills reset" (April 2026 perspective, by Thomas Mathew, VP, Head of ASEAN & Greater China)
text:
[Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work | Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > "The skills reset" (April 2026 perspective, by Thomas Mathew, VP, Head of ASEAN & Greater China)]
- Productivity at scale comes from expanding, not narrowing, the pool of people who can work with AI.
- The skills reset is a growth agenda, not just a training initiative. Three outcomes when non-specialists are enabled with real workflows (plus coaching and guardrails): productivity accelerates, innovation increases, and growth becomes more resilient because capability is distributed.
- "Trust in AI grows through use" — familiarity builds confidence, which drives adoption. Usage metrics are a useful first baseline (low usage signals an awareness, confidence, or tool-fit problem).
- Tied to Synapse (skilling 2 million people by 2030) and "AI for Impact Community Labs" hands-on enablement workshops.
- What leaders should do now: expand AI fluency beyond technical teams; prioritize hands-on enablement; empower mid-career talent; design operating models for continuous learning and distributed innovation.

doc_title: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work
section_path: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > CEO Ravi Kumar S — public positions (June 2026)
text:
[Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work | Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > CEO Ravi Kumar S — public positions (June 2026)]
- Argues AI can broaden opportunity if deployed to augment workers rather than replace them; says Cognizant has increased hiring of graduates and early-career workers (reportedly hiring 20,000+ graduates in the year).
- Describes a "flat-earth pyramid": the traditional talent pyramid flattens, with leaner middle layers but many new jobs at the "front" and "back" of AI workflows — validation, verification, and authentication roles around AI.
- Calls AI "tokenmaxxing" (maximizing token usage) a "vanity metric" — emphasizing business value over raw AI consumption.

doc_title: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work
section_path: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > Adjacent research themes (Cognizant Insights / "The Work Ahead" library)
text:
[Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work | Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > Adjacent research themes (Cognizant Insights / "The Work Ahead" library)]
- Closing the AI execution gap: two-thirds of leaders have not yet shown measurable productivity gains from AI; a quarter have paused/abandoned deployments. Firms with mature tech infrastructure plus focused AI investment are pulling ahead (billions in annual returns).
- Why "AI Builders" are essential: you can't just pull an off-the-shelf model to solve complex enterprise problems; value must be built, not bought.
- The agentic internet and "agent experience": businesses must build design, technical, and organizational capabilities for a web where AI agents are first-class participants.
- AI's two-year timeline / legacy modernization mandate: tech debt in legacy systems is now the bottleneck to AI value; a "flywheel" modernization strategy is needed.
- Think like an AI-native business; rethink the talent pyramid in the age of gen AI.
- Responsible/confident AI adoption: strong leadership commitment but gaps in business readiness; only about a third of consumers trust gen AI, so trust-building is essential.

doc_title: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work
section_path: Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > Why this matters for an ACE / AI-builder interview
text:
[Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work | Cognizant — Thought Leadership: AI and the Future of Work > Why this matters for an ACE / AI-builder interview]
Expect Cognizant interviewers to value candidates who can speak to: AI augmenting (not just replacing) people; the "tasks not roles" framing; building enterprise-grade AI rather than buying off-the-shelf; responsible/governed AI; agentic AI and the agent experience; and the legacy-modernization-as-prerequisite-for-AI argument. These mirror Cognizant's own published positions.
